english: three humours of the body (v. nyés-pa (nyes pa)) supposed to exist in nearly all the parts and organs of the body, circulating in veins of its own, producing the arbitrary and the involuntary motions, and causing various other physiological phenomena. When deranged, it is the cause of many diseases, esp. of such complaints the origin and seat of which is not known, as rheumatism, nervous affections etc. This rluṅ (rlung) or humour is divided into five species, viz.: srog-͵dzin (srog 'dzin) cause of breathing, gyén-rgyu (gyen rgyu) faculty of speaking, kyab-byéd (khyab byed) cause of muscular motion, me-mnyám (me mnyam) of digestion and assimilation, tur-sél (thur sel) of excretion; rluṅ-las gyúr-pa yin (rlung las gyur pa yin) (the disease) arises from rluṅ (rlung) Glr.; rluṅ-gis bzúṅ-ste (rlung gis bzung ste) = rluṅ-nád-kyis btáb-ste (rlung nad kyis btab ste). — These notions concerning rluṅ (rlung) are one of the weakest points of Tibetan physiology and pathology. — 4. in mysticism rluṅ ͵dzin-pa (rlung 'dzin pa) seems to be = dbugs bsgyáṅ-ba (dbugs bsgyang ba), and to denote the drawing in and holding one's breath during the procedure called gtum-mo (gtum mo) (q.v.), which is as much as to prepare one's self for contemplation, or enter into a state of ecstasy Mil.; rluṅ séms-la dbaṅ tób-pa (rlung sems la dbang thob pa) Mil., frq., is said to imply that high degree of mystical ecstasy, when rluṅ (rlung) and sems (sems) have been joined into one; he who has attained to the mgyogs-rluṅ (mgyogs rlung) is able to perform extraordinary things, e.g. with a heavy burden on his back he is able to run with the greatest speed, and the like. — Comp. rluṅ-rta (rlung rta) the airy horse, n. of little flags, frequently to be seen waving in the wind on Tibetan houses, on heaps of stones, bridges etc. The figure of a horse which together with various prayers is printed on these flags signifies (acc. to Schl. 253) the deity rta-mčog (rta mchog). Huc also mentions superstitious practices that may be called rluṅ-rta (rlung rta). — rluṅ-mdá (rlung mda') Sch. air-gun. — rluṅ-nád (rlung nad) disease caused by rluṅ (rlung), v. above. — rluṅ-dmár (rlung dmar), rluṅ nág-po (rlung nag po) prop. dust-storm, a storm whirling up clouds of dust; further: storm, tempest in general, also a gale at sea Glr. and elsewh. — rluṅ-ťsúb (rlung tshub) whirlwind, snow-storm Mil. — rluṅ-séms (rlung sems) v. above, rluṅ (rlung) 4. — rluṅ-sér (rlung ser), rluṅ-bsér-bu (rlung bser bu), rluṅ bsir-bạ (rlung bsir ba), a violent wind Cs.
1. in C.: corner, hole, place for hiding a thing; Lex.: kuṅ-bui rlubs (khung bu'i rlubs). — 2. Sch.: ditch, pit, pool, abyss, mei rlubs (me'i rlubs) fire-pool.

jaeschke: rlóg-pa, pf. brlags, fut. brlag, imp. rlog(s), brlag
english: vb. a. to ldóg-pa (ldog pa), 1. to overthrow, to destroy; tál-bar (thal bar) or rdúl-du rlóg-pa (rdul du rlog pa) to reduce to powder, to destroy entirely Thgy. and elsewh.; rtsa-ba-nas (rtsa ba nas), or rnám-par (rnam par), to annihilate, e.g. all the infidels Pth., to break, to smash e.g. a vessel C.; to lose C., *'á-ma lag-soṅ* ('a ma lag song) I have lost my mother C., *lug čig lag soṅ* (lug cig lag song) one sheep has perished C. — 2. fig. to pervert, to infatuate, nyés-pai dri-mas yóṅs-su brlágs-te (nyes pa'i dri mas yongs su brlags te) quite corrupted by the filth of sin Dzl.; čúṅ-mar ͵dzin-pai bsám-rlags-ťso (chung mar 'dzin pa'i bsam rlags tsho) those infatuated by thoughts of marriage Glr.; brlág-po (brlag po) foolish, stupid, of a little child Thgy.
sometimes erron. for kloṅ (klong) or loṅ (long).

I. adj. (Cs. 'moist') W. wet, tsaṅ-rlón (tsang rlon) quite wet, wet through; hence of meat, vegetables and the like, fresh, green, raw B. and col. — 2. vb., pf. and fut. brlan (brlan), to make wet, to moisten, čus (chus), čar-pas (char pas) Dzl. II. Sch.: to answer, with lan (lan), also glón-pa (glon pa), ldón-pa (ldon pa), blán-pa (blan pa), zlón-pa (zlon pa).

jaeschke: rlób-pa, pf. brlabs, fut. brlab, imp. rlobs
rlóm-pa I. vb., pf. brlams, fut. brlam
english: 1. to be proud of, to glory in, to boast of, with termin., bder rlóm-pa (bder rlom pa) to boast of one's good fortune, gčig-par (gcig par) or gčig-tu rlóm-pa (gcig tu rlom pa) to be proud of the identity with... Tar. — 2. to love, to adhere to, to be attached to W., to strive after, gżaṅ-gyi nór-la (gzhan gyi nor la). — 3. to be possessed, of demons, gdón-gyis brláms-pa (gdon gyis brlams pa) Lt. — II. sbst. pride, bsags kyaṅ rlóm-pas ͵kyer (bsags kyang rlom pas 'khyer) if perhaps (any merit) has been gathered, it is taken away again by pride Mil. — Deriv. rlóm-po (rlom po) a boaster, an arrogant person Cs.; rlóm-sems (rlom sems) pride, arrogance.
(Cs.) the thigh, brla ná-ba (brla na ba) a pain in the thigh Do., brla gyas (brla g.yas)
